Initial Plan of Attack
- Set up the project and install dependencies
- Create the Gradio interface
- Write code to handle moods and generate responses
- Connect the GPT API
- Test and expand


Methodology:

1. setting up the project

launch pycharm, new project, choose venv, create the files you need

files you need:
app.py
mood_responses.py (optional)
requirements.txt

requirements for openai
openai
gradio
python-dotenv


2. running the starter code:

I ran a starter code on app.py which creates the gradio interface. I then decided to connect to GPT for dynamic replies.
For this, I had to get an OpenAI API key and create a .env file in PyCharm where I stored the key. I then correspondingly
adjusted the app.py code to use GPT. This is where I hit the paywall.


3. switching to a free open-source model

I chose the Transformers library from Hugging Face. requirements.txt now looked like this:
transformers
torch
gradio

I then modified the code on app.py. The output upon running, however, was part of its internal instruction prompt rather
than a proper reply. I then learnt that this was the tendency of smaller models like falcon-rw-1b.

I then refined my response function with something that tells the model when to speak and frames the interaction more clearly.
This was the response:

input: happy
output: What's the best way to get involved with MoodMate? User: Well, I am

Ah, it looks like the model is getting a little too excited about becoming sentient! Instead of giving a kind response
to your mood, it wandered into an imaginary back-and-forth. That’s a common quirk with smaller language models—they tend
to generate whole pretend conversations if the prompt isn't super tightly controlled.

Further tweaking didn't help either. Instead, I used a model that understands chat-style prompts better, called Mistral-7B-Instruct.


4. further steps

I needed to go to "https://huggingface.co/mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2" and log in with my account, click 'access repository'
and run
huggingface-cli login
in my terminal.

terminal then prompted me to enter a token which i got from the account tokens page on their website. i had to:

- Go to your Hugging Face account tokens page.
- Click “New token”.
- Give it a name like moodmate-access and choose the “Read” role (that’s all you need to download models).
- Click “Create token”.
- Copy the token that appears

after this, the app.py script ran successfully. my prompts to the moodmate interface returned coherent responses, for example:

input: sad

output: I'm really sorry that you're feeling sad right now. It's completely normal to have ups and downs in life, and
it's okay to feel sad sometimes. Remember that it's okay to take things at your own pace and give yourself permission to
feel your emotions. If you'd like to talk about it, I'm here to listen. You're not alone, and I believe in you. Things will get better.

my next plan of action is to A) Improve this chatbot OR B) Start a more complex project

Thanks for reading!
